content.text: A model's
context window

caps how many tokens — measured as token IDs after encoding — fit in one forward pass. Input tokens (prompt, history, RAG) and output tokens (completion) compete for the same budget. Agent builders who measure tokens accurately with the correct tokenizer avoid silent truncation, surprise API errors, and cost overruns.
What it is

Context window size is a hard limit on total tokens per request, e.g. 8K, 128K, or 200K. Providers publish max input, max output, and combined caps. Tokens are counted after subword encoding — not characters, words, or bytes. A 128K window holds at most 128,000 token IDs in the attended sequence; exceeding it requires truncation, summarization, or splitting across calls.
Why it matters

Agents stack system prompts, tool definitions, retrieved documents, and multi-turn history. Each component consumes tokens; subword granularity means the same English sentence costs different counts on different models. Underestimating usage drops early messages or RAG chunks; over-reserving output tokens starves the prompt. Token-accurate budgeting is prerequisite to reliable long-running agents.
Benefits
Predictable limits
— encode-before-send gives exact headroom versus provider caps
Cost control
— pricing is per token; accurate counts enable spend forecasting
Safer RAG
— fit retrieval chunks to remaining window after fixed overhead
Debuggable failures
— context exceeded errors map to measurable ID counts
Trade-offs
Longer advertised windows increase prefill latency and cost even when not fully utilized
Reserving max output tokens reduces space available for input on combined-limit APIs
Tokenizer differences mean migration between models changes how much text fits
Special tokens and chat templates consume overhead not visible in raw user text
How it works
What counts toward the window

Typically: system message tokens, user/assistant turns, tool call blocks, RAG injected text, and generated completion tokens. Chat templates insert special tokens around roles — count the fully formatted string, not individual message bodies alone. Some APIs count input and output separately; others enforce a single combined cap.
Input versus output budget
If max_context = 128K and max_output = 4K, effective input headroom may be 124K on combined-limit endpoints. Agent orchestrators should compute:
available_input = max_context - reserved_output - current_completion
. Dynamic agents that grow history each turn must re-count every iteration.
When to use it
Before sending large RAG payloads — verify chunk token totals plus prompt overhead
When selecting models — compare effective window after tool schema size
Implementing sliding-window or summarize-and-prune policies — trigger on measured counts
Quoting cost to stakeholders — multiply token counts by per-million rates
How to implement
Measure a full agent prompt with tiktoken:

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
MAX_CONTEXT = 128_000
RESERVED_OUTPUT = 4_096

system = "You are a code review agent. Output JSON findings."
tools = '[{"name": "read_file", "parameters": {"path": "string"}}]' * 20
history = "\n".join(f"User: message {i}\nAssistant: ack {i}" for i in range(200))
rag = "Document chunk: " + "token budgeting " * 500

full_prompt = f"{system}\n{tools}\n{history}\n{rag}"
input_tokens = len(enc.encode(full_prompt))
headroom = MAX_CONTEXT - RESERVED_OUTPUT - input_tokens

print(f"Input tokens: {input_tokens:,}")
print(f"Headroom for output: {headroom:,}")
if headroom < 0:
    print("WARNING: prompt exceeds budget — truncate history or RAG")
Use Hugging Face tokenizer with chat template for open-weight models:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct")
messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You call tools when needed."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the attached logs."},
]
formatted = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
    messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
)
token_count = len(tokenizer.encode(formatted))
print(f"Formatted prompt tokens: {token_count}")
Examples

A 2,000-word PDF excerpt might encode to ~2,600 tokens with cl100k_base but ~3,100 tokens on a smaller-vocabulary model that splits technical terms more aggressively. Budgeting from word count would mis-estimate by 20%+.
Real-world example

A document-analysis agent injects ten 8K-token RAG chunks (~80K tokens) into a 128K model, plus a 15K-token tool schema and 20K conversation history — total ~115K before user message. Reserved 8K for output leaves insufficient headroom; the API rejects the request. The team adds a pre-flight encode step, ranks chunks by relevance, and fills only the remaining token budget after measuring fixed overhead once per session.
Common mistakes
Using characters ÷ 4 as a universal token estimate across models and content types
Counting user messages without chat template and special tokens
Ignoring tool schema tokens in function-calling agents
Assuming a 128K model allows 128K of RAG text plus prompt plus output
Counting tokens once at session start without re-measuring as history grows
Best practices
Pre-flight encode every request with the deployment tokenizer
Cache token counts for static system prompts and tool definitions
Reserve output tokens explicitly before sizing RAG injection
Log input/output token usage from API responses to calibrate estimates
Prefer trimming oldest history or lowest-scored RAG chunks when over budget
Performance and scalability

Prefill compute scales with input token count; very long contexts increase latency before the first output token. Token counting itself is inexpensive — run it on every orchestration turn. For high-QPS agents, avoid sending redundant tool schemas each turn by caching static prefixes where providers support prompt caching.
Comparison with alternatives

Character or word limits are rough proxies that fail on code, JSON, and non-English text. Provider-specific tokenizers (tiktoken for OpenAI, model tokenizer for open weights) are authoritative. The context-management topic covers orchestration strategies (summarization, sliding windows); this topic covers accurate token measurement that feeds those strategies.
Mental model
The context window is a
fixed-length shelf measured in token IDs
. Every prompt piece and every generated token occupies shelf space; when full, something must be removed or the request fails.
Key takeaways
Context limits apply to token IDs after encoding, not raw text length
Input and output tokens share the budget on most combined-limit APIs
Measure with the exact model tokenizer including chat templates and tool schemas
Re-count each agent turn as history and RAG content grow
Pre-flight encoding prevents silent truncation and context-exceeded errors
